citation: Biology 206: Vocabulary Definitions - Gene Mutation & Chromosomes (Chapters 12 & 13) - McGraw Hill

questions.question: What is 'aneuploidy' and what cellular process causes it?
options: a- Aneuploidy is normal chromosome number (46 in humans)
b- Abnormal chromosome number (extra or missing); caused by nondisjunction during meiosis I or II
c- Aneuploidy only affects sex chromosomes
d- Aneuploidy is a synonym for polyploidy
e- Aneuploidy cannot affect cell viability
questions.answer: b- Abnormal chromosome number (extra or missing); caused by nondisjunction during meiosis I or II

questions.question: Define 'monosomy' and give an example of a viable monosomy in humans.
options: a- Monosomy is loss of one chromosome; most are lethal except 45,X (Turner syndrome)
b- Monosomy is gain of one chromosome (same as trisomy)
c- All monosomies are equally viable
d- Monosomy only affects autosomes, never sex chromosomes
e- Monosomy cannot occur in human populations
questions.answer: a- Monosomy is loss of one chromosome; most are lethal except 45,X (Turner syndrome)

questions.question: Define 'trisomy' and identify the three autosomal trisomies most commonly seen in newborns.
options: a- Trisomy is missing one chromosome (same as monosomy)
b- Extra chromosome (47 total); Down (21), Edwards (18), Patau (13) are most common in newborns
c- Trisomy only affects sex chromosomes
d- All autosomal trisomies are equally viable
e- Trisomies cannot survive beyond the first month of life
questions.answer: b- Extra chromosome (47 total); Down (21), Edwards (18), Patau (13) are most common in newborns

questions.question: What is a 'mutation' versus a 'polymorphism' in terms of population frequency?
options: a- Mutation and polymorphism are identical terms
b- Mutation: rare DNA change affecting phenotype; Polymorphism: variant in >1% population, usually neutral
c- Polymorphisms always cause disease while mutations do not
d- Mutations only occur in genes while polymorphisms occur in non-coding DNA
e- Polymorphisms are inherited while mutations are not
questions.answer: b- Mutation: rare DNA change affecting phenotype; Polymorphism: variant in >1% population, usually neutral

questions.question: Define 'frameshift mutation' and explain why it's more severe than point mutations.
options: a- Frameshift is deletion of an entire chromosome
b- Insertion/deletion altering reading frame, changing nearly ALL downstream amino acids; point mutation changes only one
c- Frameshift mutations have no effect on protein
d- Frameshift mutations only affect non-coding DNA
e- All frameshift mutations are silent
questions.answer: b- Insertion/deletion altering reading frame, changing nearly ALL downstream amino acids; point mutation changes only one

questions.question: What is a 'mutagen' and give examples of mutagens.
options: a- Mutagen is a synonym for mutation
b- Agent causing mutation: chemical exposure, radiation (X-rays, UV), or replication errors; many are also carcinogens
c- Mutagens protect against mutations
d- Only radiation can act as a mutagen
e- Mutagens are beneficial to DNA
questions.answer: b- Agent causing mutation: chemical exposure, radiation (X-rays, UV), or replication errors; many are also carcinogens

questions.question: Define 'de novo mutation' and explain its significance.
options: a- De novo mutation is inherited from both parents
b- New mutation neither parent carries; usually from replication errors not mutagenic exposure; more likely in hotspots
c- De novo mutations cannot affect phenotype
d- All mutations are de novo
e- De novo mutations only occur in females
questions.answer: b- New mutation neither parent carries; usually from replication errors not mutagenic exposure; more likely in hotspots

questions.question: What is a 'splice-site mutation' and what are its potential consequences?
options: a- Splice-site mutation is a missense mutation
b- Alters intron removal site; can cause exon skipping or intron translation depending on which genes affected
c- Splice-site mutations cannot affect protein structure
d- Splice-site mutations only occur in regulatory genes
e- Splice-site mutations always produce beneficial proteins
questions.answer: b- Alters intron removal site; can cause exon skipping or intron translation depending on which genes affected

questions.question: What is a 'tandem duplication' and how does it occur?
options: a- Tandem duplication is deletion of genetic material
b- Two complete gene copies adjacent to each other; results from crossing over errors
c- Tandem duplication only affects chromosomal ends
d- Tandem duplications cannot be inherited
e- Tandem duplications are identical to translocations
questions.answer: b- Two complete gene copies adjacent to each other; results from crossing over errors

questions.question: Define 'pseudogene' and explain its relationship to gene mutations.
options: a- Pseudogene is a functional protein-coding gene
b- DNA sequence similar to gene but not translated; may descend from gene duplication during meiosis
c- Pseudogenes encode proteins identical to original genes
d- Pseudogenes cannot result from mutations
e- All pseudogenes become functional during development
questions.answer: b- DNA sequence similar to gene but not translated; may descend from gene duplication during meiosis

questions.question: What is a 'transposon' and what are three ways it can disrupt genomic function?
options: a- Transposon is a permanently fixed DNA sequence
b- 'Jumping gene' causing insertion mutations; can disrupt origin site, shut off target gene, or alter reading frame if not multiple of 3
c- Transposons only increase gene expression
d- Transposons never affect surrounding genes
e- Transposons are always beneficial
questions.answer: b- 'Jumping gene' causing insertion mutations; can disrupt origin site, shut off target gene, or alter reading frame if not multiple of 3

questions.question: Define 'anticipation' in the context of repeat expansion disorders.
options: a- Anticipation means the disease will be prevented in future generations
b- Expansion of triplet repeat with each generation; earlier onset and more severe phenotype in subsequent generations
c- Anticipation occurs only in maternal inheritance
d- Anticipation means phenotype improves with age
e- Anticipation cannot occur in genetic disorders
questions.answer: b- Expansion of triplet repeat with each generation; earlier onset and more severe phenotype in subsequent generations

questions.question: What is a 'copy number variant' (CNV) and when does it affect health?
options: a- CNV is a single nucleotide change
b- Sequence varying in copy numbers between individuals; affects health if disrupts genes, neutral if in non-coding regions
c- All CNVs are harmful
d- CNVs cannot vary between individuals
e- CNVs only affect forensic applications
questions.answer: b- Sequence varying in copy numbers between individuals; affects health if disrupts genes, neutral if in non-coding regions

questions.question: What is a 'karyotype' and what is its primary clinical application?
options: a- Karyotype is a mutation
b- Chromosome chart displaying chromosomes by size/structure; diagnostic tool for identifying chromosomal abnormalities
c- Karyotypes show only sex chromosome information
d- Karyotypes cannot detect aneuploidy
e- Karyotypes are only used in research, not clinical practice
questions.answer: b- Chromosome chart displaying chromosomes by size/structure; diagnostic tool for identifying chromosomal abnormalities

questions.question: What is chromosomal 'shorthand' and what information does '46,XY,del(7q)' convey?
options: a- Shorthand is unrelated to chromosome abnormalities
b- Abbreviation listing chromosome number, sex makeup, atypical autosomes; example = male missing part of long arm chromosome 7
c- Shorthand can only describe sex chromosome abnormalities
d- Shorthand is identical to karyotype formatting
e- Shorthand cannot identify specific chromosome bands
questions.answer: b- Abbreviation listing chromosome number, sex makeup, atypical autosomes; example = male missing part of long arm chromosome 7

questions.question: What is 'meiotic nondisjunction' and how does it cause aneuploidy?
options: a- Nondisjunction is normal chromosome separation
b- Failure of chromosome pairs to separate during meiosis; produces gamete with extra chromosome and gamete with missing chromosome
c- Nondisjunction only affects mitosis
d- Nondisjunction always occurs in females
e- Nondisjunction produces normal euploid gametes
questions.answer: b- Failure of chromosome pairs to separate during meiosis; produces gamete with extra chromosome and gamete with missing chromosome

questions.question: What is a 'deletion' versus a 'duplication' in structural chromosome mutations?
options: a- Deletion and duplication are identical mutations
b- Deletion: chromosome segment missing (loss); Duplication: segment present twice (gain)
c- Duplications are always more harmful than deletions
d- Deletions only occur on sex chromosomes
e- Both occur with equal frequency in human populations
questions.answer: b- Deletion: chromosome segment missing (loss); Duplication: segment present twice (gain)

questions.question: What is a 'translocation' and how does it differ from deletion/duplication?
options: a- Translocation is a point mutation
b- Two chromosomes join/exchange parts; differs from deletion (loss) and duplication (gain) by moving genetic material between chromosomes
c- Translocations only affect one chromosome
d- Translocations always cause aneuploidy
e- Translocations cannot be inherited
questions.answer: b- Two chromosomes join/exchange parts; differs from deletion (loss) and duplication (gain) by moving genetic material between chromosomes

questions.question: What is cell-free fetal DNA (cfDNA) testing and what are its advantages over invasive prenatal testing?
options: a- cfDNA requires fetal tissue sampling like amniocentesis
b- Noninvasive blood test (~20% from placenta); entire fetal genome reconstructed without infection/miscarriage risk unlike amniocentesis
c- cfDNA can only detect Down syndrome
d- cfDNA is more invasive than amniocentesis
e- cfDNA testing is less accurate than karyotyping
questions.answer: b- Noninvasive blood test (~20% from placenta); entire fetal genome reconstructed without infection/miscarriage risk unlike amniocentesis
